back_translation: When Rachel saw that she did not bear children for Jacob, she envied her sister and said to Jacob, "Give me children, or else I will die."
Jacob became angry with Rachel and said, "Am I in the place of God, who has withheld children from your womb?"
She said, "Behold, here is Bilhah my maidservant. Go in to her, that she may bear children on my knees, and through her I too may build a family."
So Rachel gave Bilhah her maidservant to Jacob as a wife, and Jacob went in to her.
Bilhah conceived and bore a son to Jacob.
Rachel said, "God has judged me and has also heard my voice and given me a son." Therefore she named him Dan.
Bilhah, Rachel's maidservant, conceived again and bore a second son to Jacob.
Rachel said, "I have wrestled mighty wrestlings with my sister, and have prevailed." So she named him Naphtali.
When Leah saw that she had stopped bearing children, she took Zilpah her maidservant and gave her to Jacob as a wife.
Zilpah, Leah's maidservant, bore Jacob a son.
Leah said, "How fortunate!" So she named him Gad.
Zilpah, Leah's maidservant, bore Jacob a second son.
Leah said, "I am happy! For the daughters will call me happy." So she named him Asher.
During the wheat harvest, Reuben went out and found mandrakes in the field and brought them to his mother Leah. Then Rachel said to Leah, "Please give me some of your son's mandrakes."

But she said to her, "Is it not enough that you have taken away my husband? Will you also take my son's mandrakes?" Rachel said, "Very well, he may sleep with you tonight in exchange for your son's mandrakes."
When Jacob came in from the field in the evening, Leah went out to meet him and said, "You must come in to me, for I have hired you with my son's mandrakes." So he slept with her that night.
And God listened to Leah, and she conceived and bore Jacob a fifth son.
Leah said, "God has given me my wages because I gave my maidservant to my husband." So she named him Issachar.
Leah conceived again and bore Jacob a sixth son.
Leah said, "God has given me a good gift; this time my husband will honor me, because I have borne him six sons." So she named him Zebulun.
Afterward she bore a daughter, and named her Dinah.
God remembered Rachel; he listened to her and opened her womb.
She conceived and bore a son, and said, "God has taken away my reproach."
She named him Joseph, saying, "May the LORD add to me another son."
After Rachel had borne Joseph, Jacob said to Laban, "Send me away, that I may go to my own place and to my own land.
Give me my wives and my children for whom I have served you, and let me go, for you know the service that I have served you."
Laban said to him, "If I have found favor in your eyes, please stay. I have learned by divination that the LORD has blessed me because of you."
He added, "Name your wages, and I will give them."
Jacob said to him, "You know how I have served you and how your livestock have fared with me.
For what little you had before me has increased greatly, and the LORD has blessed you wherever I set my foot. But now, when shall I provide for my own household?"
Laban asked, "What shall I give you?" Jacob answered, "You shall not give me anything. If you do this thing for me, I will return to feed and keep your flocks.
Let me pass through all your flocks today and remove from them every speckled and spotted sheep, every dark-colored lamb, and every spotted and speckled goat. These shall be my wages.
My honesty will testify for me later, when you come to check on my wages. Every one that is not speckled and spotted among the goats and dark among the lambs shall be considered stolen by me."
Laban said, "Agreed, let it be as you have said."

That same day Laban removed the male goats that were striped and spotted, and all the female goats that were speckled and spotted — every one with white on it — and every dark lamb, and put them in the hand of his sons.
He set a three-day journey between himself and Jacob, while Jacob shepherded the rest of Laban's flocks.
Jacob took fresh branches of poplar, almond, and plane trees, and peeled the bark, exposing the white inner wood of the branches.
He set the peeled branches in the troughs, in the watering troughs where the flocks came to drink, in front of the flocks, so that they would mate when they came to drink.
And the flocks mated in front of the branches, and the flocks bore young that were striped, speckled, and spotted.
Jacob separated the lambs and made the flocks face the striped and all the dark in Laban's flocks. He set his own flocks apart and did not put them with Laban's flocks.
Whenever the strong females mated, Jacob would set the branches in the troughs in the sight of the flocks, that they might mate among the branches.
But when the flocks were weak, he did not set them out. So the weak ones went to Laban and the strong ones to Jacob.
Thus the man became exceedingly prosperous and had large flocks, maidservants and menservants, camels and donkeys.
